coredata.dc:title: A new fluorescent probe based on metallic deep eutectic solvent for visual detection of nitrite and pH in food and water environment
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Jianqiang
Liu, Sitong
Zhao, Ning
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: Nitrite is a widely used food additive that has been shown to be carcinogenic and can cause health damage when consumed in excess. Therefore, developing a detection method is in demand. Here, we prepared a novel Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) using metallic deep eutectic solvent (MDES) which showed high sensitivity and selectivity. Besides, it also showed excellent pH-dependent luminescence characteristics, which proved the feasibility as a pH sensor. Under the optimal conditions, the detection linear of nitrite ranged from 0.2 to 80 µM, and the detection limit was 50 nM. The recovery rate was between 98.8 % and 104.1 % in food and water samples. For pH monitoring, its fluorescence intensity was linearly correlated in the pH range from 2 to 7, accompanying a unique differential solution color change of colorless-yellow-green. Therefore, it can be used as an excellent fluorescent probe for detection of nitrite and pH in food and water environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe-doped carbon dots
Metallic deep eutectic solvent
Nitrite
pH-dependent
Indicator

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and stability investigations of Fe-CDs
Optimization of synthesis conditions for Fe-CDs
Optimization of detection conditions for Fe-CDs
Possible mechanism of quenching to NO2 –
Analysis of NO2 – and pH sensing of Fe-CDs

Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image. (B) Particle size distribution of Fe-CDs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs + NO2 –.
XPS spectra of the Fe-CDs (A). Full scan spectrum, high resolution spectra of C 1s (B), O 1s (C) and Fe 2p (D).
Optimization of the detection conditions for nitrite by Fe-CDs: (A) pH value, (B) temperature (°C), (C) time (min).
Fluorescence lifetime spectra (A) and UV absorption spectra (B) (C) of Fe-CDs under nitrite or different pH value. (D) The zeta potential of Fe-CDs with and without nitrite.

Fluorescence emission spectra and linear diagram of Fe-CDs with increasing nitrite (A) (B) and pH value (C) (D). (E) and (F) visualization of Fe-CDs under sunlight and UV light in the presence of nitrite and pH value.
Determination of NO2 – in real samples by this method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A new fluorescent probe based on metallic deep eutectic solvent for visual detection of nitrite and pH in food and water environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016 Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite is a widely used food additive that has been shown to be carcinogenic and can cause health damage when consumed in excess. Therefore, developing a detection method is in demand. Here, we prepared a novel Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) using metallic deep eutectic solvent (MDES) which showed high sensitivity and selectivity. Besides, it also showed excellent pH-dependent luminescence characteristics, which proved the feasibility as a pH sensor. Under the optimal conditions, the detection linear of nitrite ranged from 0.2 to 80 µM, and the detection limit was 50 nM. The recovery rate was between 98.8 % and 104.1 % in food and water samples. For pH monitoring, its fluorescence intensity was linearly correlated in the pH range from 2 to 7, accompanying a unique differential solution color change of colorless-yellow-green. Therefore, it can be used as an excellent fluorescent probe for detection of nitrite and pH in food and water environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is commonly known as industrial salt, which is widely used as coloring agent, preservative, antibacterial agent and extensively provided as an additive in food industry (Chiesa, Arioli, Pavlovic, Villa, & Panseri, 2019). However, nitrite can ruin people's health when consumed in excess (Li et al., 2019). It can be converted into manifold nitrogen oxides in human's body and may react with protein metabolites in acidic medium such like amines to form nitrosamines which be a grave issue in carcinogenic risk (Francisca, Raquel, & António, 2020). Apart from this, excessive nitrite in the blood stream can also convert hemoglobin into methemoglobin, consequently interfering with the oxygen transport system in the body, that leading to tissue hypoxia and lower blood pressure (Wu et al., 2020). Not merely its wide application in the food industry, nitrite can also be found in fertilizers degradation, acid rain, industrial waste and so on. Therefore, ion content monitor of nitrite becomes more and more significant for the sake of people's health. It urged us to establish a higher sensitive and selective method. Moreover, with a variety of industrial pollutants, fertilizers and other continuous discharge into the soil and water environment, our lives have been seriously affected (Polat, Beyaz, & Çilingir, 2020). At this time, pH monitoring becomes particularly important due to its’s possibility of determining the pollutant in the water environment, so that excessive pollution can be reduced. For the addition of nitrite could greatly affect the pH value, it was particularly necessary to detect the stability and fluorescence performance of Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) at different pH values, which laid a foundation for its future application as a biosensor in the field of pH monitoring in water environment.

Up to date, several methods for the detection of nitrite have been developed, including chromatography (Antczak-Chrobot, Bak, & Wojtczak, 2018), capillary electrophoresis (Kalaycioglu & Erim, 2016), electrochemical methods (Vilian, Umapathi, Hwang, Huh, & Han, 2021) and spectrofluorometric (El Hani, Karrat, Digua, & Amine, 2022). The Griess-based colorimetric assay is the most commonly method among them which has been used for decades (Singhaphan & Unob, 2021). For instance, Banerjee (Kumar, Banerjee, & Chatterjee, 2012) et al reported a rhodamine-based “turn-on” type fluorogenic probe, which can be used for trace detection of nitrite ions. The method is simple, rapid, low-cost and widely used, but there are still many defects such as lower sensitivity, toxicity and easily disturbed by other stroma and anions. Chromatography and electrochemical method are popular with high sensitivity as well as good selectivity, but too time-consuming and not suitable for trace detection (Liu, Yang, Abdel-Halim, & Zhu, 2013). In contrast, fluorescence method has gradually attracted the attention of the public due to its advantages of simplicity, low cost, high sensitivity and selectivity.

For now, a great diversity of fluorescent nanomaterials are offered as fluorescence sensors, such as semiconductor quantum dots, polymer dots, fluorescent nanodiamonds, fluorescent nanoclusters and so on (Xia, Zhu, Feng, Yang, & Yang, 2019). In recent years, owing to its simple preparation, excellent optical properties, low toxicity and environmental protection, carbon dots (CDs) has consumed a growing number of people's attention (Sun et al., 2021). Nevertheless, a range of fluorescent materials that served as carbon dots are costly and deleterious. Besides, it must meet a strong acid condition for most fluorescent probes to detect nitrite ions. Therefore, it is still essential to develop a green alternative material.

At this time, a new type of solvent called deep eutectic solvent (DES) has attracted people's attention. Hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) and hydrogen bond donor (HBD) are supplied to form hydrogen bonds at a certain mole ratio so that to provide a hydrogen bond network which has been successfully applied in various research fields (Rossella, Nicolò, Cristian, & Rosanna, 2021). It has great thermal stability, high atomic economy and biodegradability (Zhou et al., 2019), in accordance with the concept of “green chemistry”, hence becoming a strong substitute for several toxic organic solvents and appealed more and more scholars by degrees.

In this work, we prepared one type of metallic deep eutectic solvent (MDES) by magnetically stirring to successfully obtain a green fluorescent probe Fe-CDs that first appeared. The fluorescence intensity was greatly quenched under the presence of nitrite ions. This method is environmentally friendly and can be put into mass production to detect nitrite in food and the environment. Not only in high sensitivity, good selectivity, but the detection limit can be as low as 50 nm. In addition, its excellent pH-dependent luminescence and unique color changes also made it expected to become a pH indicator to detect the pH of polluted water which of great environmental protection as well as low cost. According to the change of pH value to judge the degree of water pollution, and then take the corresponding countermeasures to reduce or prevent the harm to water quality in time.

The morphology of Fe-CDs was analyzed using a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (FEI, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an Escalab250Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA). A Nicolet iN10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) was applied to measure FT-IR and research functional group information of Fe-CDs. UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out on a UV-5500PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash Instruments, China). All the fluorescence spectra were obtained on a F97 (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., ltd. China). All the solutions were filtered by the Filtration Membrane (Jin Long, Material: PES (water), Pore Size: 0.22 µm). The fluids requiring dialysis were treated with the Dialysis bag (MW: 500 Da, 77 mm, USA). The freeze-drying process of synthetic materials was carried out with a LYOQUEST-85 Freeze-dryer (Azbil Telstar Technologies S.L.U, Spain). All the solutions were stirred using a 78HW-1 thermostatic magnetic stirrer (Shanghai hannuo Instrument Co., ltd. China).

dl-malic acid was purchased from Bide Pharmaceutical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycerol, glucose, lactic acid and saccharum were obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Tianjin, China). MnCl2, CoCl2, ascorbic acid, l-cysteine and glutathione were offered by McLean Biochemical Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). FeCl3, MgCl3, KCl, NaCl, NaNO2 and Na2SO3 were purchased from Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Tianjin, China). NaNO3 and Na2CO3 were obtained from Kemio Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Tianjin, China). Na2SO4 and citric acid were acquired from Bodi Chemical Co., ltd. (Tianjin, China).

In this experiment, MDES was synthesized by referring to the previous classical magnetic agitation carried out by mole ratio feeding method (Ali, Tuzen, & Kazi, 2020; Yoo et al., 2018), but with a slightly optimized on the synthesis conditions (molar ratio of materials, temperature, time). MDES was prepared by using dl-malic acid as the HBA, glycerol and Fe for HBD at a proper molar ratio (dl-malic: glycerol: Fe, 2:8:1) without any other solvent to the conical flask. MDES was prepared by using dl-malic acid as the HBA, glycerol and Fe for HBD at a proper molar ratio (dl-malic: glycerol: Fe, 2:8:1). Next, we used a regular heating magnetic stirrer and the solution was mixed at 80 °C for 30 min to obtain MDES, a homogeneous and transparent liquid phase. It can be reserved in a closed container and used after cooling naturally to room temperature.

A novel Fe-CDs was prepared that applied to the detection of nitrite and pH value in food and environment for the first time by traditional hydrothermal method (Arumugam & Kim, 2018) using MDES, and the synthesis conditions were optimized (synthesis time, temperature). 5 mL MDES in precision weighing was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water with ultrasonic for completely mixing. After that, the mixed solution was placed into baking oven at 200 °C for 6 h. Until cooling naturally to room temperature, the product was filtered by a 0.22 µm filter membrane, dialyzed for 24 h with dialysis bags (MW:500 Da), and then underwent a freeze-drying for 48 h. Fe-CDs were finally obtained.

30 mg Fe-CDs was put into 100 mL volumetric flask in accurate weighing and added to a constant volume to get the stock solution. 20 µL Fe-CDs (30 µg·mL−1) was blended with 200 µL NaNO2 solutions at different concentration (1–200 µM) in centrifuge tube and made a constant volume to 1 mL with PBS (pH = 2).The reaction solution was followed a fluorescence test after a constant temperature water-bath-shock for 20 min. In the meantime, a selective test was also necessary. It was noteworthy that fluorescence test operation was carried out according to following conditions. The excitation wavelength was at 410 nm, and the fluorescence value at 475 nm was used as the determination value of NO2 –. The emission slit width was 10 nm.

To explore the fluorescence response of Fe-CDs in different pH values, Fe-CDs was placed into solutions with distinct pH values under correct test conditions. 20 µL Fe-CDs (30 µg mL−1) were placed in 1 mL centrifuge tubes with different pH (adjusted with HCl and NaOH). A fluorescence test was then followed after mixing evenly. The excitation wavelength was at 410 nm, and the fluorescence value at 475 nm. 10 nm was the suitable slit width.

We first pretreated the samples according to the literature reports and modified them slightly (Wang et al., 2016). The river water used in the experiment came from Shahe River (Benxi, China), tap water was taken from our laboratory, and all the food was purchased from the local supermarket of Benxi. The specific operation method was as follows: Exact weighing 5.0 g pickles, bacon and sausage, crushed them with a juicer and placed them in a beaker. 14 mL borax solution (5 %) and 100 mL distilled water (70 °C) were then put into it and mixed them. After well mixed, heated in boiling water for 15 min, 5 mL potassium ferricyanide and zinc acetate solution were added to precipitate the protein. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, adjusted the total volume to 200 mL and filtered with filter paper first. All samples were then filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane and stored at 4 °C for analysis.

NaNO2 standard solution (10, 30, 50 µM) was then loaded into each sample solution respectively to detection, as described in “3.3” for nitrite. The recoveries and relative standard deviations were calculated followed. Besides, in order to verify the specificity to detect nitrite, the response of Fe-CDs sensor to other interfering ions (anions, metal cations, common food additives) was either tested (Liu, Yang, Abdel-Halim, & Zhu, 2013).

Fe-CDs prepared in this paper displayed terrific fluorescence properties and favorable dispersibility. The morphology, composition and surface structure of Fe-CDs were analyzed by Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope. As shown in Fig. 1 A, there was no obvious aggregation in solution and Fe-CDs was uniformly dispersed. As illustrated in Fig. 1 B, we can clearly find the size of Fe-CDs was mainly distributed within the range of 5 nm, with an average particle size of 3.72 nm. In addition, the HR-TEM images (inset in Fig. 1 A) indicated that the clear crystalline structure of parallel crystal planes of Fe-CDs were observed with a carbon lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was similar to the interplanar spacing of graphene (Zhu, Bai et al., 2017).

As displayed in Fig. 1 C, the infrared spectrum of Fe-CDs was obtained by FT-IR. The black line represented Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs + NO2 – the red line, which were applied to identify various possible functional groups. Measurements showed a significantly strong and wide absorption band at 3423 cm−1, which could be put down to the stretching vibration of OH (Lu et al., 2015). It was mainly owing to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds by the large number of hydroxyl groups on dl-malic acid and glycerol used for the synthesis of DES, so that furnished a great quantity of hydrogen bond systems. Beyond that, the out-of-plane bending vibration of OH led to absorption peaks of Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs + NO2 – at 1398 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1, which further confirmed the existence of hydroxyl groups (He et al., 2018). The infrared absorption peak at 1633 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of carbonyl CO, while the peak at 1041 cm-1can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C-OH (Panyong et al., 2021), declared that there may be carboxyl group in the structure (Liu, Xue, Liu, Wang, & Wang, 2018). The peak at 626 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of the CH bond. In the light of the above characterization results, it can be inferred that the surface of Fe-CDs was rich in oxygen-containing groups. These hydrophilic groups helped to greatly improve the hydrophilic stability of Fe-CDs in the aqueous system, and enabled to chelate with N and O elements due to the doping of metal iron (Liu, Xu et al., 2022), accordingly provided more active action sites for nitrite sensing.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, elemental composition and chemical bonding of Fe-CDs were as well analyzed with X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS), revealing that Fe-CDs was primarily composed of C, O and Fe, which was well consistent with the FT-IR results. The three characteristic peaks that centered around 285, 583 and 710 eV, could be ascribed to the binding energies of C 1s, O 1s and Fe 2p, demonstrating the successful doping of Fe. Fig. 2 B was the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, presented three fitting peaks at 284.0, 285.1 and 287.2 eV, belonging to CC (sp2-hybridized carbons), CO and CO (Deng et al., 2021). This indicated sp2-hybridized carbons consisted in the graphite structure of the prepared Fe-CDs, and also confirmed the existence of carbonyl functional groups. As plotted in Fig. 2 C, the three fitted peaks at 530.1 eV, 531.1 eV and 532.2 eV correspond to three oxygen-containing functional groups, namely CO, OH and CO bonds, respectively (Arul & Sethuraman, 2018; Sun et al., 2021). And as presented in Fig. 2 D, the absorption peaks at 710.8 and 722.2 eV in the high-resolution electron energy spectrum of Fe 2p explained the phenomenon iron in oxidation state. All the above results better confirmed the successful doping of Fe as well as the creation of many oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of Fe-CDs (Zhuo et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability investigations of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of Fe-CDs were verified by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. We found that Fe-CDs was also very soluble in water without any chemical modification. As shown in Fig. S1 A, Fe-CDs solution presented a transparent yellowish color in sunlight, while showed bright blue fluorescence under excitation of 410 nm with UV lamp. UV–vis spectra (black lines in Fig. S1 A) showed different characteristic absorption bands of Fe-CDs including two UV absorption peaks at 241 and 351 nm attributed to π-π* electron transitions of the CC bond and n-π* transitions of the CO bond, respectively. As displayed in Fig. S1 B, fluorescence properties were investigated at different excitation wavelengths from 350 nm to 520 nm to figure out whether Fe-CDs was excitation wavelength dependent. The results implicated that at 410 nm excitation wavelength, the maximum emission wavelength of Fe-CDs was about 473 nm, and could change with the excitation wavelength, showing obvious dependence. At the same time, the absolute quantum yield (ABS-QY) of Fe-CDs was calculated at 5.59 %. The effects of high ion intensity and radiation time on the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs were also discussed. Fig. S1 C showed that fluorescence intensity was basically stable at NaCl solution that concentration up to 1 M (Sun et al., 2021). As be seen in Fig. S1 D, being radiated under 410 nm UV lamp for 90 min, the fluorescence intensity of MDES could still remain above 75 % (Liu, Wei et al., 2022). These results indicated that Fe-CDs could be used as a stable and excellent fluorescent probe for sensing determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions for Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, a new fluorescent probe was successfully prepared by doping metal in DES for the first time. The best fluorescence conditions of Fe-CDs were obtained by detecting the fluorescence intensity of different doping metals, types, synthesis temperature and time (Li et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. S2 A, in the presence of nitrite, the quenching degree of Fe-CDs doped with Cu, Mn, Fe, Zn and Co was investigated separately. Except for the slight quenching of Zn and Co probes, only the Fe-CDs composed by Fe-DES made significant quenching. Different colors of Fe-CDs in sunlight were expressed in the illustration. To verify this effect, we also compared the fluorescence probe synthesized exactly by malic acid and ethylene glycol without metal doping, and obviously found no quenching effect on nitrite. This clarified that the fluorescence probe prepared by metal-mediated DES was in excellent sensing performance. As shown in Fig. S2 B, in the investigation of metal element doping mole ratio (0.2–1.2), the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs increased significantly until the mole ratio reached 1, but slowly at 1.2. To sum up, 1 was finally determined as the best molar ratio of Fe doping. Fig. S2 C showed the change of fluorescence intensity at 120–200 °C. It could be seen that at 120–160 °C, the fluorescence intensity was quite weak, but gradually rose from 160 °C and at 200 °C the maximum, which may be related to the physical properties of Fe-CDs. The effect of synthesis time on the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs could be seen in Fig. S2 D. Distinctly the fluorescence intensity grew with the synthesis time by degrees, and the enhancement at 6 h evidently, yet changed slightly after 6 h. By way of saving time, we chose to react for 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the best analytical performance of fluorescent materials, we studied a series of detection conditions and tested the performance of Fe-CDs in detecting nitrite at different solution acidity, reaction time and temperature. All optimization experiments were carried out three times. Fig. 3 A indicated the influence of pH value. The fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs decreased rapidly with pH value from 2 to 7, and disappeared when reached 7. Therefore, pH = 2 was selected as the optimal condition. In addition, based on the pH-dependent luminescence properties of Fe-CDs, we also designed a pH sensing experiment (Wang et al., 2021). As can be seen from Fig. 3 B, the quenching degree of nitrite on Fe-CDs increased with the temperature, indicating that the reaction speed was affected by high temperature, and 60 °C was the final reaction temperature. Fig. 3 C was the changing trend of fluorescence intensity with time after nitrite added (Qu, Chen, Jiang, & Zhao, 2021). It can be observed that fluorescence quenching existed at 473 nm, 59 % quenching occurred at 5 min, and as high as 96 % after 20 min, indicating that the reaction has been completed at 20 min, then was selected as the best reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of quenching to NO2 –
ce:section.ce:para: In order to look into the highly selective detection of NO2 – by Fe-CDs and to better understand the quenching mechanism, we talked over the fluorescence lifetime, UV–vis absorption spectrum and FT-IR spectrum of Fe-CDs after the addition of NO2 –. When NO2 – was inserted into Fe-CDs at 410 nm excitation wavelength, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs at 473 nm decreased significantly, and the fluorescence spectrum had a slight red shift. At the beginning, the fluorescence lifetime of Fe-CDs before and after nitrite addition were studied by steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer. As we can see in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence life of Fe-CDs was sharply shortened with the increase of NO2 – concentration (3.76 ns → 1.72 ns). After nitrite added, the UV absorption peak of Fe-CDs at 241 nm was only weakly enhanced, indicating that no new complex was formed. The results could be seen in Fig. 4 B. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of Fe-CDs was mainly caused by dynamic quenching, which meant that the excited state molecules of the fluorescent substance lost their excitation energy and returned to the ground state through the mechanism of energy transfer or charge transfer through the collision with the quenching agent molecules in the process. Subsequently, we further validated the nitrite quenching mechanism and found that the ultraviolet absorption peak of nitrite did not overlap with the fluorescence spectrum of Fe-CDs, thus ruling out the possibility of energy resonance transfer (FRET) (Jiang et al., 2021). The disappearance of the infrared absorption peak at 1041 cm−1 and the narrowing at 3423 cm−1 were observed in FT-IR spectra after the addition of nitrite, which was due to the reaction between nitrite and the CO bond as well as OH and other oxygen-containing groups on the surface of Fe-CDs, which destroyed the rich hydrogen bond system (Ren et al., 2020). So, we speculated that the charge transfer occurred due to the previous interaction between the two. As can be seen in Fig. 4 D, the potential value of Fe-CDs increased from −8.45 mV to −0.15 mV after the addition of NO2 –, indicating the existence of an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process (He et al., 2020; Su, Teoh, Wang, Liu, & Chang, 2017). Fe-CDs with low electronegative surface group acted as electron donor (D) and interacted with nitrite as electron acceptor (A) to form a D-π-A conjugated system (Li et al., 2021). The fluorescence excitation of this system resulted in charge transferred from electron donor to electron acceptor, then the color of Fe-CDs solution changed from white to yellow, and the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually, thus causing the fluorescence quenching phenomenon, which showed in Fig. 5 E (Zhu et al., 2017). The ability of D or A to push and pull electrons may also be affected by the addition of objects to be measured, which may cause the red shift of fluorescence spectrum in the experiment.

Except as the excellent fluorescence sensing of NO2 –, Fe-CDs also had a unique pH-dependent characteristic. The UV absorption spectrum and fluorescence lifetime were studied to explore the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. S2 A, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs decreased largely with the pH value from 2 to 7, and approached to 0 when pH = 7. Accordingly, the pH value of Fe-CDs was adjusted to 6, and the fluorescence life was basically unchanged before and after nitrite addition. It was inferred that the fluorescence quenching of Fe-CDs was caused by static quenching (SQE). To further verified this conjecture, the changes of UV absorption peaks of Fe-CDs at different pH values were also studied. As we can see in Fig. 4 C, the absorption peak at 351 nm was enhanced with the increase of pH value, accompanied by an evident red shift phenomenon. Once pH value up to 7, the UV absorption peak of the newly formed substance completely overlapped the excitation spectrum of Fe-CDs, corresponding to the result of fluorescence lifetime. We hypothesized that SQE was caused by the formation of new aggregates of intermolecular/intramolecular hydrogen bonding of Fe-CDs with the increase of pH value, which led to the increase of electrostatic repulsion between Fe-CDs (more carboxyl groups were deprotonated) (Qu, Chen, Jiang, & Zhao, 2021). As the result of this, Fe-CDs would be unstable when the pH value increased, which was manifested as the color of Fe-CDs solution changed from white transparent to green suspension, with the fluorescence quenching phenomenon. The specific color change process could be seen in the illustrations in Fig. 5 F.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NO2 – and pH sensing of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In accordance with the optimal conditions previously, the response ability of Fe-CDs to nitrite and its pH was studied. We first discussed the fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs at different concentrations in Fig. 5 A. As illustrated in Fig. 5 B, we learned the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs decreased apparently with the increase of nitrite concentration (0–300 µM) (Li et al., 2020). It performed a good linear response in the range of 0.2–80 µM (y = -39.62 [NO2 –] + 4212, R2 = 0.9932). Based on the pH sensitivity characteristics of Fe-CDs, we constructed a pH fluorescence sensing platform, and the fluorescence spectrum was shown in Fig. 5 C. With the pH value ranging from 2 to 7, obviously quench attacked to the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs, and accompanied by a significant change in the color of the solution, colorless-yellow-green, which greatly enabled the sample to be a color indicator for pH detection. By observing Fig. 5 D, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs also had an outstanding linear curve within the range of pH 2–7 (y = -923.2 [pH] + 6660, R2 = 0.9931). In contrast with other detection methods, Fe-CDs had a better linear range and showing high pH sensitivity that was considered to be appropriate for detecting nitrite and pH in food and the environment. Table S1 showed other detection methods for nitrite.

In purpose of learning the selectivity of the Fe-CDs sensor platform for nitrite monitoring, we summarized the changes of fluorescence intensity after adding some common anions, metal ions and additives under the same experimental conditions. As plotted in Fig. S3 A, five times the concentrations of interfering substances were mixed into the Fe-CDs system, like anions (F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, OH–, NO3 –, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, CO3 2–, CH3COO–, HCO3 –), metal ions (Fe3+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+) and some additives (citric acid, lactose, sucrose, glutathione, etc.) (Li, et al., 2019). Only nitrite emission peak at 475 nm was observed with the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply, and conspicuous transformation also occurred to the Fe-CDs (from blue to colorless). No fluorescence change happened to the other ions in evidence. Anything else, it can also be seen from Fig. S3 B that Fe-CDs fluorescence intensity was slightly affected when carried out the interference test under the coexistence of nitrite and interfering ions, which resoundingly verified the high selectivity of Fe-CDs for nitrite detection.

A new fluorescence assay for nitrite detection was put forward in this study. So as to assess the feasibility of the fluorescent probe in actual sample detection, a criterial addition method was projected to verify nitrite levels in real water samples and food (Li et al., 2019). The interference of particle turbidity in the experiment could be eliminated by 0.22 µm membrane filter. It can be seen from Table 1 that nitrite existed in nearly all samples except under water environment, and there was no significant difference between the standard addition method and our proposed method. The recoveries of food and water samples were 98.8 %–104.1 % and 99.9 %–103.8 %, RSD less than 3.2 % and 3.5 %. The results above showed the method had reliable accuracy and precision, and the prepared Fe-CDs could be used for the analysis of actual samples, which had a good application prospect in environmental, especially in food detection.

In conclusion, we acquired a simple and economical green fluorescent probe (Fe-CDs) based on Fe doping using Fe-CDs as raw material which had good stability, water solubility and responded specificity to NO2 –. Until now it had already been successfully used for the detection of nitrite in food and water environment. The possible quenching mechanism of NO2 – by this probe was fluorescence dynamic quenching, caused by the charge transfer between electron receptor NO2 – and low electronegative oxygen-rich groups on the surface of Fe-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of nitrite in the range of 0.2–80 µM, and the detection limit (LOD) was 50 nM. The excellent selectivity of Fe-CDs brought great potential to fluorescence sensing. It was worth noting that, found on the mechanism of static quenching, Fe-CDs was also very sensitive to the change of pH value, and a more efficient fluorescence quenching effect could be achieved by adjusting pH value which its fluorescence intensity was linearly correlated in the pH range from 2 to 7. Moreover, accompanying a unique differential solution color change of colorless-yellow-green that did a great help in further proved that Fe-CDs could be selected as an excellent candidate for pH sensor, for quick indication in water environment. It also laid a foundation in other environments such as the detection of organisms that indeed has a very bright development prospect.

Jianqiang Song: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sitong Liu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Ning Zhao: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Longshan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.133935.
